data.title: Shake Shack: Near-Term Outlook Is Better Than Expected, But Valuation Is Stretched
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Shake Shack Inc. (NYSE:SHAK) with a downgrade to a hold rating, and that was a major mistake. I thought the macro backdrop was going to be a major headwind to SHAK, but that turned out to be not the case. The near-term outlook is far better than I previously assumed. That said, not everything is rosy yet, and with valuation now looking stretched, I still don’t think the risk/reward is attractive. Therefore, I continue to stay neutral for now.

Growth Outlook Is Brighter Than Previously Anticipated

Previously, I was concerned about the tough consumer spending environment impacting SHAK, but SHAK has proven me wrong, and I now have a much more positive view on the near-term performance. Most notably, new unit development is gaining real momentum that is very encouraging. SHAK is not just on track to meet guidance; they are seemingly doing better than expected, so much so that management has raised the guide, now targeting 45 to 50 new company-operated Shacks in FY25, which would be SHAK’s largest class of openings ever. What makes this more impressive is that these new stores are less capital intensive. Despite cost pressures from inflation and potential tariffs, SHAK is reducing build costs by at least 10% y/y. This tells me that SHAK is growing revenue better than expected, and costs are reducing as expected, which paints a very positive picture on earnings growth.

What was also encouraging was the clear success of new drive-thru formats, especially in suburban growth markets. In the recent call, management highlighted record-breaking opening day sales at several drive-thru stores in the Southwest. The implication here is more than just a validation of the new drive-thru formats working, but that SHAK has found a new avenue to drive growth, one that also helps it diversify its dependence on core markets like NYC and LA (both of which are facing more economic headwinds). This diversification, paired with a working store format, is a plausible key driver for near-term top-line.

In addition, SHAK has also managed to capture demand from menu innovation. Take the Dubai Chocolate Pistachio Shake, for instance. Despite its $8.49 price point (relatively expensive vs. a burger that costs ~$10), it was sold out quickly where it was offered, signalling that demand for premium, differentiated items is still there. Similarly, the Summer BBQ menu expansion (from two sandwiches to four) and the drive-thru combo strategy are good examples of how SHAK is executing its menu innovation strategy well.

I also have more confidence that the new loyalty program (I was cautiously optimistic about this previously) should work better than I expected because of the new guest recognition platform (successfully launched in 1Q25). With this platform, SHAK can deliver more targeted incentives, such as multi-visit challenges (this is an effective way to increase visit frequency and deepen guest engagement).

Lingering Concerns

Even with the very positive developments/data points, some of the concerns behind my previous hold rating are still valid, and while I am turning relatively bullish, I am still leaning towards neutral for now. The macro uncertainty hasn’t gone away. In fact, management acknowledged a wider range of uncertainty in the current environment when they gave their updated FY25 guidance. The clearest sign of this caution is the more conservative view for same-store-sales growth, now expected to be low single-digits [was +3% previously]. Also, it is also true that traffic was down 4.6% y/y in 1Q25, and while April showed some sequential improvement later in the month, same-store-sales growth for April still ended at -1% y/y.

Also, the concern I had about new unit performance is still not off the table yet. SHAK’s accelerating pace of openings is encouraging, and they’re surely doing a good job controlling build costs. But average weekly sales fell 1.4% y/y to $72,000, and this suggests that new stores are not scaling fast enough. I have to see solid improvement here in the next 1 or 2 quarters to be convinced that these openings can contribute meaningfully to the P&L; if not, they are just a drag on growth as fixed costs go up. The G&A line in the P&L clearly shows that what I am saying is materializing (slower new store growth leads to operating deleverage that pressures margins). For reference, in 1Q25, G&A (excluding one-time items) increased 100 bps as a percentage of revenue, to 12.3%.

Valuation

Even if I wanted to turn bullish on the stock because of the encouraging developments above, valuation is not cheap. The market has reacted very quickly to the positive developments, driving valuation up to 24x forward EBITDA from 17x previously. At 24x, this is at the high of the past three years' trading range and is also above the average of 21.8x. My take here is that the market has already priced in the positives, and for valuation to be even higher, SHAK has to perform better than the already upgraded consensus estimates (refer to the chart below). This is not impossible, but as I mentioned above, there are still visible weaknesses that could hurt earnings growth. As such, I am holding back from upgrading to a buy.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating to SHAK. The business is doing a lot of things right, but some near-term risks are still present. Traffic trends remain soft, newer units need to ramp faster, and higher fixed costs are resulting in operating deleverage. At 24x forward EBITDA, the stock is already pricing in much of the good news. I think the setup could turn bullish if unit volumes improve and macro headwinds ease, but for now, I prefer to wait.

data.title: Arcutis Biotherapeutics: Navigating Commercialization With Promising Upside
data.description:
I usually recommend proof of concepts or product market fits in biotech bets to cut the regulatory and approval risks and wait for market response feelers. The risk to cutting that risk is the run-up in share prices post those proofs. Arcutis Biotherapeutics (NASDAQ:ARQT) has visibly made decent progress in taking out quite a few unknowns and now the share prices stand at a point where it is hard to gauge whether the market has overrun its fundamentals, particularly with significant pipeline possibly being optimistically priced in already. I will try and find out how ARQT is lined up from here after some consolidation post the breakthrough rally in late 2023 to early 2024.

What Arcutis Ticked Recently

Partials approvals for the Arcutis' leading product, Zoryve cream, had started rolling in from mid-2022. However, share prices had continued to be hammered in a broader market sell-off and as markets waited for more indications from Arcutis for a buy. The inflection point came in late 2023 due to a mix of factors. A $102m public offering was closed, which was good for cash burn concerns and also showed management's confidence in its financials improving. The period also coincided with FDA approvals for uses that opened up the roflumilast platform's second commercial avenue. Revenue increases from 2022 along with a good outlook into 2024 (and a cash flow breakeven possibility in 2026) made for an overall compelling growth narrative. Since then, Arcutis has been inching up quietly as it has continued to deliver good topline increases, seen significant markets opening up after further usage approvals of the roflumilast platform and struck key strategic partnerships.

Financial Progress

Operating losses were closing the gap to profitability at a decent pace to take out cash burn concerns till the end of 2024. However, Q1 2025 losses have tracked back, on stagnating revenues while R&D and marketing costs have been on the rise. I ignore the marginal increase in R&D costs as a temporary and insignificant trend - the larger trend of reducing R&D costs is actually supportive of the company's movement into commercialization phases. Marketing costs are expected to increase as Arcutis expands market penetration. Most importantly, the revenue QoQ drop (though marginal) is partly attributed to a $4.1 million non-recurring reduction in reserves for product returns added to Q4 2024 revenues. Overall demand trends look good, with prescription demand growth noted.

Although the blip appears temporary, if I approximate the net loss as the cash burn rate, the available liquid cash buffers are sufficient to last only 1–2 quarters. That is, if revenues and margins don't pick up from here. While the threat of debt or equity dilution from fund raises cannot be ruled out (after significant dilution in the past as seen in the jump in outstanding shares), I am more convinced of revenue and margins progressing well with approved new markets already tested.

Arcutis has maintained its conviction to be cash positive by 2026 even in its Q1 2025 Earnings Call. Keep the net loss as a key metric to watch for the next quarter or so, but no panic yet. This is not an unusual situation for an early commercialization stage biotech firm. And Arcutis has tested markets at least and is also in a strong breakeven trend. The inclusion of short-term investments in the cash buffer total makes the total buffer size ~$200m, almost two years of cash assuming stagnation in growth. The reason I emphasize on dilution risks and hence the runway, is because as Arcutis keeps trading through the commercialization phase, the growth and early-stage effects may get less influential on EPS and dilution risks will start impacting EPS more.

How the Pipeline Could Play Out

Two extensions of current offerings are in promising commercialization stages. The scalp and body PsO foam has already been approved, and AD Peds use for age groups 2-5 is expected to open up later this year. The combined impact is not a multiplier for the top line but significant milestones, increasing a further million in terms of target patients. The AQR-255 topical treatment for alopecia areata and ARQ-255 biological treatment are additional pipeline supports in the near term.

The more significant market opens up when Arcutis moves meaningfully into the dermatologist treated patients market (from commercial use right now), addressable through Medicare and Medicaid coverage. Significant gains are also expected from the Kowa partnership, initiated in July 2024, to co-promote Zoryve across primary care and pediatric settings, expanding reach beyond dermatology clinics. These developments could multiply the market reach to 5-6 times more patients, and much of the efforts are underway already.

It is also encouraging to note the pipeline impacts a large total addressable market by prescriptions, not even considering increasing transitions from steroids to effective topical treatments.

Competition and Expansion

The market for dermatology therapeutics is competitive with several alternatives. The core product, Zoryve, appears well protected with patents, but alternative treatments in the future could impact the moat. Arcutis emphasizes on better safety profiles for its treatment line as an offering benefit to differentiate itself from competition. Overall, Arcutis' market share in the space is also in a material uptrend, allaying competitive pressures on its growth.

In terms of expansion too, Arcutis appears well poised with key partnerships in China, Japan and direct sale in Canada set to increase the topical roflumilast platform's reach. This expands Arcutis' top line and addressable market as well.

Valuation

Relative to historical EV to forward revenues, ARQT is trading at reasonable valuations. Arcutis' multiple of 4.8x is notably higher than Incyte's 2.2x, reflecting its earlier stage status, higher growth expectations and single product focus with significant upside potential. While Incyte's lower multiple reflects a mature, diversified business with lower risk, Arcutis's premium is justified by its rapidly expanding Zoryve sales, cleaner safety profile and promising pipeline. However, this valuation already prices in moderate success, so upside depends on strong market adoption and successful international expansion.

Investment Advice

I believe Arcutis has made meaningful progress in de-risking key regulatory and market uncertainties by securing multiple approvals and expanding its commercial footprint. The company's early revenue traction, coupled with strategic partnerships in important international markets like China, Japan, and Canada, position it well for growth. While near term financials show some volatility in losses and cash burn concerns, the underlying trend towards breakeven by 2026 remains credible, supported by a reasonable cash buffer.

The pipeline extensions and expanded market access, especially via the Kowa partnership targeting primary care and pediatrics, could significantly broaden Arcutis' patient reach beyond specialist dermatology, amplifying sales potential. Competition remains a factor, but Arcutis' safer topical profile and patent protections provide a meaningful moat.

Valuation at 4.8x EV/forward revenue appears reasonable, reflecting both early-stage risks and growth opportunities. However, it also builds in execution and growth to a good degree. Overall, Arcutis is a compelling speculative growth biotech play but needs continued monitoring for revenue momentum and execution (expansion and pipeline). Investors should monitor dilution risk and cash runway during this commercialization phase. Speculative Buy positions in Arcutis now should be well-supported. Expectations are moderate returns (compared to the rally seen in the past couple of years) over the next year or two as further execution risks are taken off - valuations already bake in some success.

data.title: Trump Ignores The Markets' Warning At Our Economic Peril
data.description:
Volcanoes are generally preceded by warning signs in the form of seismic movements, gas emissions etc. Those warning signs give governments time to take preventive policy action to limit the damage.

Something similar might be said of economic crises. They are generally preceded by warning signs from the markets. Those warnings give governments time to take remedial policy action to reduce the risk of a crisis. Now that several markets are flashing warnings of real economic trouble ahead, the Trump administration would be well advised to take them seriously and dial back its aggressive import tariff and tax cutting policies.

Start with the strange behavior of the government bond market. Normally, when the Federal Reserve reduces its short-term interest rate, one would expect long-term government bond yields to fall on the expectation of lower inflation. One would also expect long-term bond yields to fall at a time of market turbulence and at a time of investor de-risking.

However, the opposite has been occurring of late. Whereas since September 2024, the Fed has cut its lending rate by a full percentage point, the all-important 10-year Treasury bond yield has risen by three quarters of a percentage point to its present level of around 4.5 percent. And it has done so notwithstanding a high degree of stock market volatility.

Equally unusual has been the dollar’s behavior. Normally, when a country imposes high import tariffs on its trade partners, that country’s currency appreciates as other countries seek to weaken their currencies to restore competitiveness.

Also, when there is world stock market turbulence, one would expect the dollar to appreciate as investors sought the safe haven of the US Treasury market. Yet, since the start of the year, the dollar has depreciated by 8.4 percent. And it has done so despite a whole array of US tariffs on our trade partners to levels that have not been seen in the past 100 years.

The gold market is also flashing signs of a loss of foreign confidence in our economy. Since the start of the year, the gold price has increased by a third. In part, this has reflected an increasing trend of foreign central banks to shift away from the dollar towards gold in their international reserve holdings. It also has reflected growing fears of long run inflation.

The United States can ill-afford to raise its foreign creditors’ concerns. Foreigners not only hold $9 trillion of the $29 trillion of US Treasury bonds outstanding. They also are called upon to finance a sizable part of the government’s budget deficit.

That is presently running at around $1.9 trillion or 6.25 percent of GDP, and it is on a rising trajectory. This makes it all the more concerning that the Trump Administration seems to be going out of its way to undermine foreign investor confidence.

One way it is doing so is by creating investor uncertainty through its import tariff policy. With alarming frequency, large tariff increases have been announced only to be paused for trade deals to be worked out.

One example of this was the “liberation day” announcement on April 2 of punitive reciprocal import tariffs to be applied to about 60 countries by April 9. That was to be over and above a 10 percent baseline tariff to be applied to all countries.

However, buckling to a sharp adverse reaction in both the stock market and the US government bond market, Trump announced a 90-day pause in the application of those tariffs. More than halfway through that 90-day pause, only one trade deal with the United Kingdom has been struck. Who knows what will happen on July 9 when that pause is due to end?

Another way that the Trump administration is undermining foreign investor confidence is by pushing its “One Big Beautiful Bill” through Congress. That is raising the specter of resorting to inflation to deal with the country’s debt problem.

It is estimated that at a time when public debt is already growing, if implemented, that bill will add $3 trillion to the budget deficit over the next decade. In addition, that bill includes a clause that has to be sending shivers down foreign investors’ spines. According to Section 899, the US Treasury can impose additional taxes of up to 20 percent on income earned by foreign entities from countries that enact taxes deemed “unfair” to US interests.

Rudi Dornbusch, the late MIT economist, said that economic crises take longer to occur than one would have thought possible. However, when they do occur, they occur more quickly than one would have thought probable. With markets already flashing warning signs, the Trump administration would do well to pay heed to Dornbusch’s dictum and make a major economic policy course correction.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Targa Resources: Underappreciated Resilience Supports Upside
data.description:
Shares of Targa Resources (NYSE:TRGP) have been an excellent performer, gaining 40%. However, while the broader market has regained its early April losses, Targa is a notable underperformer, with shares essentially sitting flat over the past two months. Relative to the pipeline sector, Targa has a relatively low dividend and elevated valuation, which seem to be weighing down the stock amid increasing growth fears. Now is a good time to see if this recent underperformance provides an opportunity to buy into a growth stock at a good price, or if further underperformance is likely.

Targa is a leading midstream company focused on natural gas liquids (NGLs) and associated products. Historically, this business has been riskier than other pipeline businesses as much of its earnings were tied to commodity prices, rather than the fee-based revenue streams that dominate transportation contracts. This higher risk is why its dividend track record is not as robust and steady as some peers. In recent years, Targa has worked to de-risk its business, and it has built a leading franchise in the high-growth Permian Basin, able to take NGLs from the wellhead to the export market. As a result, over the past five years, it has grown EBITDA by 24% as Permian volumes have increased by 23%.

Targa has primarily done two things to improve the risk profile of the business. First, the company has reduced its financial risk by prioritizing deleveraging since 2020. With lower leverage, its financial position can withstand underlying volatility in results better. TRGP now has $16.2 billion of debt, or 3.6x debt/EBITDA leverage, which is well within its 3-4x range.

Additionally, it has worked to restructure contracts to reduce the sensitivity of financial results to commodity prices. About 90% of its business is now fee-based. As a result, a 30% decline in commodity prices is an $80 million headwind, while there is a $130 million benefit from a 30% jump in prices. There is an asymmetric commodity payoff given the nature of its hedges and cost recaptures in its contracts. Indeed, as noted on the earnings call, commodity prices are already below fee floors on some contracts.

In the company’s first quarter reported on May 1st, we saw the benefits of these shifts. Adjusted EBITDA increased by 22% to $1.2 billion. Midstream fees rose by 9% to $677 million while net sales of commodities were $874 million from $727 million last year, excluding hedge movements. Given volume growth, operating expenses rose by 9% to $304 million

Looking at segment results, its gathering and processing (G&P) unit increased its operating margin by $46 million to $602 million, thanks to ongoing growth in the Permian. Permian volumes grew by about 11% from last year, though they were down sequentially by 1%. In other territories, volume fell by 6% sequentially and 5% from last year. There was particularly harsh weather in January and February, which disrupted gas volumes. This has been a fairly common theme across Q1 results, from firms like Hess Midstream (HESM) and MPLX (MPLX), and so I would not overreact to these declines. I expect a return to growth in the coming quarters.

Its logistics and transportation (L&T) segment grew by $115 million to $647 million, given growth in NGLs and increased export activity. The same winter weather pressures were seen in Targa’s NGL and export volume throughput, which were both down sequentially. Still, NGL volumes were up solidly from a year ago, reflecting ongoing momentum across its system. Targa was also doing periodic turnaround work at its fractionation facilities in Q1, which temporarily reduced capacity. With that work complete, we should see a rebound in volumes going forward.

Targa has been a major beneficiary of increased US energy exports, given its ability to move product from the Permian all the way to Gulf Coast export facilities. Over the past eight years, LPG demand has grown by 5% as it replaces coal as a key source of heating supply. It is expected to grow 4% over the next 15 years, thanks primarily to growth in Asia.

The US has seen outsized growth to become the leading exporter thanks to our low-cost basins. As such, we have not only grown exports dramatically, but we have significantly expanded market share. We have a fairly diverse export base. Japan and South Korea are about a quarter of exports, while China is 16%. Targa has seen some trade flow alternations, with some cargos going to SK/Japan instead of China amidst trade tensions. To the extent the Administration can strike trade deals, increased purchases of US energy exports is the easiest way for nations to reduce their trade deficit. Targa and LPG exports will be likely beneficiaries of this.

Now, while Targa has limited its commodity price risk, it has indirect risk insofar as lower prices lead to lower volumes through its system. Through April, even with lower commodity prices, management is not yet seeing its customers shift their drilling plans, given some of these long-term demand drivers. Additionally, the company maintained its 2025 guidance.

Targa is engaged in a large growth capital program, though this is funded out of operating cash flow. Indeed, in Q1, TRGP generated $328 million in adjusted free cash flow. Cap-ex spending will stay elevated at least through mid-2026, as some projects underway will not be complete until then. After that, if we do see a weaker production outlook, we could see Targa meaningfully trim its capital budget.

Given its strong cash flow and on-target balance sheet, TRGP is returning capital to investors. Over time, Targa aims to return 40-50% of cash from operations to shareholders. It has done $214 million of buybacks year to date, including $125 million during Q1. Thanks to buybacks, there has been a 2.2% share count reduction over the past year. Additionally, in April, Targa boosted its dividend by 33% to $1.00, and shares now yield 2.4%, substantially below the 6-9% yields often seen in the pipeline space. Targa has a mixed dividend track record. During the COVID crisis, Targa slashed its dividend to focus on improving its debt-laden balance sheet. Since then, it has gradually restored its dividend. With this most recent increase, its dividend is finally back above pre-COVID levels.

With its de-risked business and financial profile, I view a repeat of that dividend experience as highly unlikely, even in a similar downturn. Still, given the fact that its business has more direct and indirect commodity exposure, I expect it to maintain a smaller dividend and use more buybacks than midstream peers. That is because it is easier to use to modulate the level of buybacks based on cash flow movements, whereas the dividend is intended to be a through-the-cycle commitment.

This year, I expect TRGP to generate $3.6-$3.7 billion of distributable cash flow and ~$900 million to $1 billion of free cash flow. That is about$16.70-17.00 of DCF/share and $4.25-$4.50 of free cash flow/share. Shares have a ~10.5% DCF yield but just a 2.7% free cash flow yield, given how massive its growth program is this year—it alone should boost potential results by 5-6%.

Frankly, I think Targa has built a more durable business than appreciated, and as this is realized, I believe shares can recover. Additionally, if we see TRGP begin pulling back on growth spending next year, there is room for substantial further dividend increases and share buybacks.

At shares 52-week high, they were essentially trading at an 8% DCF yield, which would be $212 at $17 of DCF. Frankly, I do not view that as unreasonable given my view we should see ongoing export growth, supporting increased volumes through TRGP’s system over time. That said, its relatively low payout ratio is likely to limit upside, as investors in this space do seek high dividend income.

Plus, there is likely to be an overhang from lower commodity prices and TRGP’s historic sensitivity. Given these factors, I am applying a 10% discount to my fair value target and view $190 as a more reasonable price target. That still points to 17% upside, and as Targa delivers on guidance, I believe we can see valuation expand. As such, I rate the shares a buy.

data.title: Celestica: Significantly Overvalued For A No-Moat Company
data.description:
Executive Summary

A couple of weeks after my bearish Celestica (NYSE:CLS) (TSX:CLS:CA) thesis, it aged quite well as the stock price declined from around $91 to $66. There was a rebound closer to the resistance level $90 after bottoming out below $70. Everything went quite well with my bearish outlook, but the Q1 earnings release was a big positive catalyst, which made the stock rally from around $90 to $120+. Therefore, it might look that my previous thesis did not age well. I would disagree with it because Celestica is currently even more overvalued than it was in March. For a company with razor-thin margins, I think that such a generous premium over the fair value is not justified. Therefore, I downgraded Celestica's stock from SELL to STRONG SELL.

CLS Analysis

I want to start with the biggest positive catalyst that happened over the last three months, which was the Q1 earnings release. There was a double beat against consensus estimates, which the stock market met with a massive optimism. Another reason that caused a 30% rally over the last few weeks after the earnings release, was the upbeat FY2025 guidance.

There was a $150 million upgrade to the FY2025 revenue forecast, together with a more optimistic adjusted operating margin and EPS. Despite the expected improvement in core profitability metrics, the company's free cash flow outlook remained the same at $350 million. In my opinion, a 1.4% ($10.85 billion/$10.70 billion) revenue guidance is not that significant, especially taking into account that this improved revenue outlook did not affect the FCF forecast.

I do not deny that Celestica's operating margin has improved significantly over the last three years. However, it is also fair to say that the initial value of circa 3% was extremely low. Thus, despite the operating margin almost doubling over the last three years, it is currently still quite low at around 6%. Moreover, the operating margin is becoming more volatile with the growth trend becoming not so obvious. This might indicate that the operating margin expansion potential nears its peak.

Therefore, expectations of Wall Street analysts regarding Celestica's EPS growth might be exaggerated. I am saying it because the company's bottom line growth is expected to significantly outpace revenue growth over the next three fiscal years. The EPS growing significantly faster than revenue means that the company should demonstrate its significant operating margin improvements further. This might be an unfairly optimistic expectation, as we saw that Celestica's operating margin has not been that rapidly growing over the last couple of quarters.

Another indication of the assertion that Celestica's operating leverage is near its peak is the fact that the company's gross margin is not expanding. Despite quite aggressive revenue growth over the last few quarters, the gross margin has been quite stable at around 10%. Moreover, there is no clear trend of the gross margin improvement, as the metric stagnates within a quite narrow range.

Relatively low gross margin does not give much room to invest in innovation. For a company with annual sales expected at around $11 billion, around $20 million in R&D spending per quarter looks like nothing. With such low innovation spending, Celestica does not have much technological moat and pricing power. This should be another notable warning sign for investors.

The fact that Celestica currently provides a good selling opportunity is also demonstrated by insider activity. There were zero open market buys by insiders over the last twelve months, which is a screaming bearish indication. At the same time, insiders were selling quite aggressively over the last twelve months.

The assertion that insiders might see little upside is also supported by the average target price from Wall Street, which is very close to the actual current share price. Even with all the aggressive EPS expansion forecasts (which we saw previously) from Wall Street, their average target price indicates a tiny upside potential.

CLS Valuation

Such aggressive net insider selling is also highly likely to indicate significant CLS overvaluation. Let's check it by simulating a DCF model. As we mentioned earlier, the FY2025 FCF guidance is $350 million. This will be the starting point of the DCF model. My pessimistic outlook means that the DCF should be balanced out with aggressive assumptions. Therefore, I apply the FCF CAGR in line with optimistic EPS projections from consensus. A 3% growth rate into perpetuity also looks like a good choice to balance out my bearish analysis.

The discount rate is 11.56%, which is the outcome of applying CLS-related variables into the CAPM formula.

With around 22% FCF CAGR for the next five years and a very optimistic (for a no-moat company like Celestica) 3% growth rate into perpetuity, the stock is 41% overvalued.

Potential Drawbacks

There are some fundamental positives as well, which might work against the bearish opinion. Apart from a (4.77) STRONG BUY Seeking Alpha Quant rating, there is also a powerful momentum. Furthermore, the EPS revisions trend remains quite bullish. Since long-term EPS revisions from Wall Street analysts lead to adjustments to fair value calculations, potential new EPS revisions will support the bullish stance of the market.

Celestica's financial position is also a positive factor that might potentially unlock new revenue growth or profitability expansion opportunities. Celestica's total debt is not very high, below 10% of the market cap. This means that the company boasts solid financial flexibility and highly likely can easily raise several billions in debt to finance a new large project or acquisition.

Besides company-specific strengths, there might be some favorable external tailwinds as well. For instance, The U.S. tech giants might announce new aggressive AI capex spending plans, which will potentially lead to more orders for CLS and will also be met with big optimism.

The Verdict

For a company with a 10% gross margin and no apparent moat, Celestica's valuation is extremely inflated. With tiny R&D investments, it will be impossible to build a strong competitive advantage, meaning that Celestica's recent financial success is unlikely to be sustainable. As a result, downgrading this massively overvalued stock to STRONG SELL looks very logical.

data.title: Civitas Resources: Debt Maturities Should Be Manageable After Latest Bond Offering
data.description:
Civitas Resources (NYSE:CIVI) issued $750 million in new unsecured notes to pay down part of its credit facility debt and give itself plenty of liquidity to deal with its October 2026 notes.

Civitas is still aiming to pay down its net debt to under $4.5 billion by the end of 2025 and looks capable of generating over $700 million in free cash flow during the last three quarters of the year.

Civitas's value is negatively affected by weaker commodity prices, but I am still estimating its value at $54 per share in a scenario with current strip (low-$60s oil) until the end of 2026 followed by $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.

New Unsecured Notes

Civitas is issuing $750 million in new 9.625% unsecured notes due 2033. The net proceeds from those notes are going to help pay down Civitas's credit facility debt, which was at $1.05 billion at the end of Q1 2025.

The borrowing base for Civitas's credit facility was reduced from $3.4 billion to $3.3 billion recently, but this is still well above the elected commitment of $2.5 billion.

Civitas is aiming to reduce its net debt to $4.5 billion (or less) by the end of 2025. If it achieves that goal, it will have no outstanding credit facility debt until it redeems its 2026 notes.

Civitas will have $4.85 billion in outstanding notes after issuing the $750 million in 2033 notes. It has $400 million in 5.0% unsecured notes due October 2026 that it will be redeeming next year. The low interest rate on those notes means that there is no hurry to redeem them early.

Civitas also amended its credit facility to remove the springing maturity that would have moved its credit facility maturity up to April 2026 if its October 2026 notes were still outstanding.

Civitas's next note maturity (after October 2026) is in July 2028 now. Civitas's liquidity is improved by its recent notes, but it will have approximately $390 million per year in note interest (not including its October 2026 notes).

Q1 2025 Results

Civitas's Q1 2025 production (oil and total) was within its expected ranges, albeit towards the lower-end of those ranges. It had total production of approximately 311,000 BOEPD, compared to guidance for 310,000 to 320,000 BOEPD during the quarter.

Civitas's oil production ended up at roughly 141,000 barrels per day, compared to its guidance for 140,000 to 145,000 barrels of oil per day. Civitas noted that there was a modest impact to its Q1 2025 volumes due to winter weather and windstorms, which was enough to push production towards the lower-end of expectations.

Civitas also reported $495 million in capital expenditures during the quarter, which was towards the lower end of the $490 million to $520 million guidance range. This was attributed to some DJ Basin capex shifting into Q2 2025 due to activity timing.

Civitas's cash operating expenses ended up at $11.39 per BOE during Q1 2025. This included $0.43 per BOE in short-term items such as severance costs, repair and maintenance costs due to severe weather and third-party water takeaway issues in the Permian Basin (for its Holly development). There was a contractor who was unable to fulfil their water takeaway obligations, resulting in Civitas needing to spend on alternatives. Civitas expects to recover some of those additional costs from the contractor, though.

Without those short-term items, Civitas's cash operating expenses would have been around $10.96 per BOE, still a bit above its $10.30 to $10.80 per BOE guidance range. Civitas expects significant reductions in cash operating expenses per BOE later in 2025, though.

Civitas ended up generating $171 million in adjusted free cash flow in the quarter, which allowed it to pay its base dividend as well as repurchase 1.5 million shares (for $71 million).

Cost Reductions

Civitas is aiming to reduce costs by $100+ million per year in 2026, including $40 million in savings during 2025. This is expected to be from a combination of improved capital efficiency, reduced G&A costs and reduced operating expenses.

A cost savings of $100 million per year has close to the same impact on Civitas's results as a $2 per barrel increase in oil prices.

Updated 2025 Outlook

Civitas continues to aim for approximately 330,000 BOEPD in average production during 2025, including 152,500 barrels of oil per day.

At current strip prices of roughly $64 WTI oil and $3.80 NYMEX natural gas, Civitas is projected to generate $4.708 billion in revenues for 2025. This is inclusive of $158 million of positive hedge value.

Civitas has hedges covering around 48% of its oil production during the last three quarters of the year.

I'm continuing to model Civitas's cash operating expenses and capex at the midpoint of its (unchanged) full-year guidance, despite the $40 million in expected 2025 savings.

It expects a significant decrease in cash operating expenses in the second half of the year, but those costs during the first half of the year are expected to be higher than initially anticipated, as discussed above.

Civitas would need to average roughly $9 per BOE in cash operating expenses during the second half of the year to hit the midpoint of its full-year guidance.

Civitas appears more likely to end up below the midpoint of its capex guidance than its guidance for cash operating expenses.

Civitas is now expected to generate around $884 million in free cash flow during 2025.

It had approximately $5.1 billion in net debt at the end of Q1 2025. If it puts all of its free cash flow (after its $0.50 per-share quarterly base dividend) towards debt reduction, it may end up with around $4.6 billion in net debt.

Civitas is also targeting $300 million in 2025 asset sales (likely from the DJ Basin), although that will depend on whether Civitas can get what it believes is a fair price for its assets.

With a $300 million asset sale, Civitas may be able to reduce its net debt to around $4.3 billion by the end of the year, without accounting for any additional share repurchases or variable dividends.

Notes On Valuation

Civitas's Q1 2025 results were a bit on the weaker side, although much of that was due to short-term impacts from severe weather and third-party water takeaway issues.

Lower commodity prices have a noticeable impact on Civitas's estimated value, given that it has a considerable amount of debt.

However, I believe its debt is manageable in my base case scenario, which involves current strip until the end of 2026 and then $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas after that time.

I'd estimate Civitas's value in that scenario at $54 per share now. This assumes that it can reduce (and keep) its cash operating expenses below $10 per BOE going forward.

Conclusion

Civitas looks to be in decent shape with its various debt maturities after bolstering its liquidity with new unsecured notes due 2033. It does have a considerable amount of net debt (currently around $5.1 billion) but also looks capable of generating over $700 million in free cash flow during the last three quarters of the year (at slightly over $60 WTI oil).

I believe Civitas's shares should have considerable upside if oil can rebound to $70 by 2027 and in that scenario I estimate its value at around $54 per share. Civitas's cost-cutting measures should also help it to generate a significant amount of free cash flow at the current strip of around $60 WTI oil in 2026.

data.title: CorMedix: DefenCath In Exponential Growth Phase With Robust Market Position
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am bullish on CorMedix Inc. (NASDAQ:CRMD) due to its promising niche market and strong financials. The stock is undervalued, warranting a buy recommendation. The company’s product pipeline points to its bright outlook, as the company looks set to capitalize on the growing health expenditure in the US by exploring new products.

Despite its beta of 1.71, meaning the stock is almost 2x the market’s volatility, its attractive price return of more than 140% over the last year, beating the S&P 500 significantly, makes it a good pick. Its investment appeal is bolstered by its growth trajectory and potential.

Company Profile

CRMD was founded in 2006 with its headquarters in Berkeley Heights, New Jersey. The company is a biopharmaceutical which specializes in therapies for life-threatening diseases. The core of its business is its lead product DefenCath which was approved by the FDA in 2023. It is a novel antimicrobial catheter lock solution developed to reduce CRBSIs in adult patients with kidney failure receiving hemodialysis through CVCs. The company operates through one segment (Drug Product).

Financials: Tremendous Growth & Profitability Path

One thing that impresses me about this company is its financial progress, transitioning from a pre-revenue biotech to a profitable organization in 2025. In Q1 2025, net revenue came in at $39.1 million, driven by solid DefenCath sales. This was a significant leap compared to the 2024 FY total revenue of $43.5 million. Further, its net income for the quarter was $20.6 million translating to an EPS of $0.32, a stark contrast to its $14.5 million net loss in Q1 2024. The company’s operational efficiency was underscored by its adjusted EBITDA of $23.6 million and positive cash from operation of $19.7 million.

It is important to note that Q4 2024 was the first quarter for this company to achieve profitability with an EPS of $0.22, beating analysts’ estimates of about $0.17. This implies that the company has maintained two successive quarters of positive net income, an indication of its firm grip on its profitability route. Its commitment to maintaining profitability is reflected in its improved efficiencies, as demonstrated by its decreasing costs. For instance, IN Q1 ’25, its sales and marketing expenses went down by 29% to $4.5 million from $6.3 million the previous year.

Looking at its revised H1 2025 revised net revenue guidance of $70 million; I see the company’s confidence in its future growth, which is reflective of strong orders and a growing market penetration. This being the first and only FDA-approved antimicrobial catheter lock solution in the US, I see a strong growth potential here. It is estimated that the US has 250,000 to 500,000 CLABSI patients annually, with a mortality rate between 10% and 30%. This leads to an additional $300 million to $2.3 billion in annual healthcare burden in the US. Given that this product is in its early adoption stages and the company is expanding its reach, I anticipate an exponential growth trajectory in the coming quarters in this company’s financials, an optimism I share with other analysts.

I will end this section by estimating the company’s financials for the next three years. Guided by the 807.9% 3-year revenue CAGR and the fact that 2024 was the first year for the company to report meaningful revenue after the approval of its DefenCath in November 2023, the following are my growth assumptions:

  • 2025: I assume a 235% revenue growth, which reflects the product's high adoption in its early stages in the market.
  • 2026-2028: I assumed growth rates of 60%, 35% and 30% respectively, an indication of normalizing demand/ growth curve/market saturation. I believe the growth cycle will be this fast because this is the only FDA approved antimicrobial catheter lock solution in the US and therefore its adoption rate is likely to be high. Given these assumptions, below are my estimates of financials.

From my projection, I expect the company to grow its revenue to about $409 million by 2028 which is within the range of $335 million estimated by Seeking Alpha and the $429 estimated by Wall Street analysts as shown above. Above all, I expect the company to remain profitable, maintaining a healthy EBITDA margin. I think its exponential revenue growth and slowing growth in operating expenses will catalyze its profitability.

Market Position & Competitive Edge

CRMD serves in the biotech industry, precisely targeting the dialysis market. In the US, about 35.5 million people have kidney problems and almost 555,000 are on dialysis. The growth in kidney patients is alarming, with approximately 14% of the American adults affected. The Grand View Research projects that the US end stage renal disease market will grow by a CAGR of about 13% by 2030, further supporting the strong growth in kidney patients.

Most of the patients rely on central venous catheters, leaving them susceptible to CRBSIs. CRMD’s lead product addresses this critical unmet need of reducing the incidence of CRBSI infections.

The company has a robust market presence, securing commercial agreements that cover ~60% of the US outpatient dialysis centers. The product's high efficacy than similar products demonstrated by about 71% reduction in dialysis related infections gives it a competitive edge. Given its high market penetration in the US outpatient dialysis and its high efficacy, I believe this company has secured a strong foothold in the market. While larger players like AstraZeneca (AZN) are viewed as key competitors, this company’s niche focus (catheter lock solutions) offers a competitive advantage.

Above all, the product’s unique formulation which combines taurolidine and heparin offers a novel approach to infection prevention. It has no direct competitor currently approved for the same condition in the US market, giving it a first-mover advantage.

Valuation

Guided by the company’s strong revenue growth and outlook, I will value this stock based on PS ratio on a comparative basis. CRMD has the highest PS ratio among its peers, AVDL and CRON, and one would be quick to judge that the stock is overvalued.

However, I think the stock premium valuation is justified when we look at the companies’ respective revenue growth, where CRMD has grown exponentially due to its new product.

Based on these two metrics, I have one consideration in mind, and that is CRMD’s product life cycle. Although the current premium pricing seems justifiable, I don’t expect the company to maintain such a strong growth curve in the coming years and as such, I believe its PS ratio could fall as its revenue growth decelerates along the company’s product life cycle. As such, I expect its PS to fall within range with its peers although slightly above them because I expect CRMD to have higher growth rates than the peers despite my anticipated cooling growth. As such, I assume a forward PS of about 6x to estimate my price target for 2028.

Using my estimated revenue of $409 million by 2028 and the 6x forward PS, I project a market cap of about $2454 million. Assuming the current outstanding shares of 67.82 million, my price target for 2028 is about $36.

Risks

Market Adoption-Despite the high initial sales for DefenCath, its long-term success depends on sustained adoption by dialysis providers and health facilities. In case of a slowdown in market penetration, its revenue and the entire financial health could be severely impacted.

Stock Volatility - The company being a small-cap biotechnology is prone to price swings driven by market sentiments, and clinical updates. Its high beta implies high losses should the sentiments turn negative.

Reliance on a single product: Given that its lead product is the only commercialized offering the company has, any negative developments on it be it price weakness or regulatory hurdle could prove detrimental for this stock.

Conclusion

As I conclude, I reiterate my buy rating of this stock given its strong revenue growth and robust market position. Its lead product is in the infant stages of the market, with a massive potential considering the lack of a direct competitor. This leaves ample room for growth and value creation.

data.title: ADP National Employment Report: 37K Private Jobs Added In May
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The economic mover and shaker this week is Friday's employment report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. This monthly report contains a wealth of data for economists, the most publicized being the month-over-month change in Total Nonfarm Employment.

The forecast for the forthcoming BLS report is that 129,000 jobs were added in April. However, each month a few days before we receive the highly anticipated jobs report, ADP releases their data on new nonfarm private jobs.

The ADP employment report revealed that 37,000 nonfarm private jobs were added in May, down from 60,000 in April. This is the lowest level since March 2023 when there was a reduction of 53,000 jobs. The latest reading was lower than the expected 111,000 addition.

Here is a visualization of the two series over the past twelve months. There is no correlation between the ADP and BLS employment report.

Here is an excerpt from Wednesday's ADP report press release:

"After a strong start to the year, hiring is losing momentum. Pay growth, however, was little changed in May, holding at robust levels for both job-stayers and job-changers.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in the ADP headline number since the company's earliest published data with the new methodology in 2010. This is quite a volatile series, so we've plotted the monthly data points as dots along with a six-month moving average, which gives us a clearer sense of the trend. The latest six-month moving average is 115,000, the lowest level in ten months.

As we see in the chart above, the trend peaked in September 2015 and then went negative for the first time in late 2019, just before the NBER declared a recession start. The COVID-19 pandemic has brought employment numbers down to levels we have never seen this century. The trend reached a new high in 2021 at 875,000 and has recently dropped back to pre-pandemic levels.

ADP Employment: Industry

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into two categories: Goods Producing and Service Providing. The US is predominantly a services economy, so it comes as no surprise that Services employment has shown stronger jobs growth.

It makes sense that service-producing employment has plummeted during the pandemic for a couple of reasons - our economy is mostly supported by service-producing jobs, and during the pandemic, those same services were brought to a halt. In May, goods-producing jobs fell by 2,000 while service-providing jobs added 36,000.

The sector with the largest growth in April was Leisure & Hospitality, with a gain of 38,000. The biggest loser last month was Professional & Business Services, with a loss of 17,000 jobs.

The next chart is a linear representation of each industry's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Region

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into nine regions (census divisions). The region with the largest growth in May was the Mountain region, with a gain of 35,000 private jobs. Meanwhile, the West South Central region experienced the largest loss at -44,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each region's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

ADP Employment: Establishment Size

ADP also gives us a breakdown of Total Nonfarm Private Employment into five sizes. Establishments with 50-249 employees were the only group that saw growth in May, gaining 51,000 jobs, while those with 20-49 employees experienced the largest loss at -7,000.

The next chart is a linear representation of each establishment size's contribution to overall employment from the start of the series.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NIO: Don't Give Up Just Yet
data.description:
Electric vehicle start-up NIO (NYSE:NIO)(OTCPK:NIOIF) missed estimates for its first fiscal quarter on Tuesday, despite strong demand for its EVs and a ramp in ONVO deliveries, which include the popular L60 SUV. NIO is one of the largest and fastest-growing Chinese EV companies in the market, but the company suffers from high operating losses and unfortunately reported a Q/Q drop-off in its vehicle margins, a crucial performance metric for electric vehicle start-ups. Despite the short-term setback, I believe NIO is executing well against its growth targets, and the EV maker announced that it will enter more European markets in the future. Shares of NIO are unreasonably valued, in my opinion, with a price-to-revenue ratio of 0.48X, which is well below the industry group average P/S ratio.

Previous rating

I rated shares of NIO a strong buy in my last work on the EV company in March -- Massively Discounted EV Value -- as I saw a strong case for a material long-term delivery ramp. The company’s latest brand launch, ONVO, has been widely successful and although deliveries dipped at the beginning of the year due to the Chinese New Year holidays, NIO’s deliveries have picked up again. I see a favorable long-term investment setup for NIO and will continue to buy the dips along the way.

NIO missed estimates for Q1’25

NIO reported weaker-than-expected results for its bottom and top line for the first-quarter on Tuesday: the EV enterprise reported an adjusted loss of $0.42 per-share, which missed the consensus estimate by $0.05 per-share. Revenues surged $1.67B, but nonetheless missed the average prediction by $56.1M.

NIO’s delivery ramp and margin profile

NIO has had considerable success with the launch of its low-cost ONVO brand, which is making a significant delivery contribution to the EV company. NIO delivered 42,094 electric vehicles in the first-quarter, showing 40.1% growth year-over-year. This delivery boost is widely attributable to the successful launch of NIO’s low-cost ONVO brand last year in September and the popularity of the brand's first model, the L60 SUV, which appeals mostly to a younger, more price-conscious buyer demographic and is a Tesla (TSLA) Model Y competitor.

Of the 42,094 electric vehicles delivered in Q1’25, 35% of them were ONVO-branded vehicles… which is not bad for a brand that started deliveries barely eight months ago. According to NIO’s latest delivery update, the EV marker delivered 23,231 electric vehicles in the month of May, which marked a growth rate of 13.1% year-over-year.

NIO's deliveries tend to drop off in the first two months of the year as manufacturing facilities across China close for the Chinese New Year holidays. With NIO seeing strong adoption of its ONVO-models, I am optimistic that the EV maker has considerable delivery potential in 2025 and beyond.

NIO generated 12.0B Chinese Yuan ($1.7B) in revenue in the first-quarter, showing a year-over-year growth rate of 21.5%. While the EV company did reasonable well in terms of revenue growth and deliveries, given the seasonal delivery drop-off at the beginning of Q1, NIO unfortunately saw its vehicle margins decline quarter-over-year. The company’s vehicle margins contracted 2.9 PP quarter-over-quarter to 10.2%, but they still increased 1.0 PP year-over-year.

However, NIO’s rivals did much better in terms of vehicle margins. For comparison, Li Auto (LI) reported Q1 vehicle margins of 19.8% (+0.5 PP Y/Y) while XPeng (XPEV)’s vehicle margins reached 10.5% (+5.0 PP Y/Y). In terms of margins. Therefore, Li Auto still has a considerable lead on its EV rivals, with XPeng having the weakest margin profile. NIO had the second-highest vehicle margins, but was the only company in the industry group here that saw a sequential drop-off in margins, in large part due to high costs of production.

What is not looking so great is NIO’s net loss picture, as the company continues to lose a ton of money on its electric vehicle manufacturing and no major change is in sight. In the first-quarter, NIO lost as massive 6.8B Chinese Yuan ($930M) which marked an increase of 30% compared to the year-earlier period. NIO’s losses were caused mainly by higher R&D expenses as well as selling and marketing activities. Li Auto is the only electric vehicle maker in the industry group that is already widely profitable and therefore, in my opinion, the lowest-risk choice for EV investors.

Guidance for Q2’25, export strategy

NIO expects to deliver 72,000 and 75,000 electric vehicles, which implies a growth rate of between 26% and 31% year-over-year. Key to the company’s growth will be its ability to ramp up ONVO deliveries, which the company has been doing so far quite successfully, as well as international expansion in the longer term.

In a separate announcement, NIO on Tuesday said that it plans to enter markets in Europe -- including Austria, Belgium, Czech Republic, Hungary, Luxembourg, Poland, and Romania -- with its NIO-branded SUV and sedan models, such as the EL6 and the ET5. Additionally, the company seeks to penetrate new markets through its brand firefly, which is focused on high-end compact EVs. These new market entrants could be a catalyst for NIO to accelerate its delivery growth going forward and ultimately help the EV maker reduce its high operating losses.

In April, NIO said that it will bring firefly-branded EVs to 16 markets in 2025, meaning the compact EV brand is going to play a critical role in the company's expansion drive. Therefore, investors should expect not only an aggressive export push into other markets, mainly Europe, but also see a more diversified product mix in NIO's brand portfolio.

NIO’s valuation

NIO has generated significant growth in the last several years, and the company’s revenues are expected to continue to grow rapidly in the years ahead. Currently, analysts expect the EV company to generate $16.4B in revenue in FY 2026, which implies an estimated year-over-year growth rate of 29%. Between FY 2025 and FY 2030, analysts expect NIO to generate 12% average annual top-line growth, with its increasingly export-driven business model being crucial to the EV company meeting such expectations.

Shares of NIO, in part because of the company's consistent profitability issue, are trading at a big discount to the firm's longer term P/S ratio: NIO is priced at 0.48X forward (FY 2026) revenue... which represents a 70% discount to NIO's 3-year average price-to-revenue ratio.

The industry group average P/S ratio is 0.9X and implies that shares of the EV maker are valued at a 46% discount to the industry group average. As I said above, NIO has the second-highest EV margins in the EV start-up group, but the weakest earnings profile (it has the highest net losses), which is likely what caused NIO's discount to the average P/S ratio.

Under the condition that NIO can gradually lower its losses going forward, mainly through ramping up of deliveries and lowering of variable costs, I can see a path for the EV maker to revalue to the industry group average price-to-revenue ratio of ~0.9X which gives the company about 86% revaluation potential. While NIO has an inferior profitability setup and is focused on expansion, I believe in the long term the EV maker will be able to improve its earnings trajectory and therefore benefit from a higher valuation multiplier.

Risks with NIO

The biggest risk for NIO, as I see it, relates to a potential increase in competition for low-cost EV brands which could have a negative margin impact not only for NIO, but for other players in the Chinese electric vehicle market as well... especially with big companies like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) aggressively cutting costs across their entire fleet: BYD lowered costs for its EVs by up to 34% at the end of May, potentially pressuring other EV companies to follow suit in order to defend their market shares. What would change my mind about NIO is if delivery momentum for ONVO vehicles stalled, vehicle margins continued to slide, or if the EV maker failed to narrow its high operating losses.

Final thoughts

NIO did not as great as expected in the first fiscal quarter, despite solid growth in deliveries, as the EV maker is still reeling from higher losses, driving by high expenses in R&D as well as selling and marketing. The electric vehicle company also did not so great in terms of vehicle margins, which saw a sequential drop-off to 10.2%. In terms of earnings, the situation could be a little bit better as well, in my opinion, as NIO still posts significant quarterly net losses while other EV companies, like Li Auto, are already solidly profitable. However, NIO's big discount to the industry group P/S ratio compensates for this weaker earnings profile, and I see a path for a major revaluation if the EV maker continues to scale non-NIO branded EV deliveries in both China and in Europe going forward.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
